title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Shinylive is a way to run Shiny applications directly in a web browser. Traditional Shiny apps usually need a server to run the R code behind the app. Shinylive is different because it allows the app to run on the client side, meaning much of the work happens inside the user’s browser.
This is important because it changes how Shiny apps can be shared. Instead of always needing a live server, Shinylive can make it easier to publish and distribute interactive R applications as part of a static website.



The biggest difference between Shiny and Shinylive is where the app runs.
A traditional Shiny app usually runs on a server. The user interacts with the app in their browser, but the server processes the R code and sends the results back. This works well for many professional apps, but it requires hosting and server resources.
Shinylive allows the app to run in the browser using WebAssembly. This means the app can work without a traditional Shiny server. The app files can be hosted as static website files, which makes sharing simpler in some situations.



Shinylive is useful because it makes interactive apps easier to share. For example, a student, instructor, analyst, or coworker could publish an interactive R example on a website without setting up a separate Shiny server.
This is helpful for teaching and demonstrations. Instead of asking someone to install R, open RStudio, and run code locally, Shinylive can let them interact with the app directly in the browser.
Shinylive can also be useful for portfolios. A person can show an interactive R project on a website and let viewers test the app themselves.



One major benefit of Shinylive is easier publishing. Since the app can run as part of a static website, it can be easier to share through tools like Quarto websites and GitHub Pages.
Another benefit is accessibility for the audience. The viewer does not need to install R or run code on their own computer. They can open the page and interact with the app in the browser.
Shinylive can also reduce the need for server maintenance. Since the app does not rely on a traditional Shiny server in the same way, there may be fewer hosting concerns for small examples, teaching materials, or portfolio projects.



Shinylive also has limitations. Since it runs in the browser, it may not be the best choice for very large datasets or complex applications that require a lot of computing power.
It may also take longer to load because the browser has to load the tools needed to run the app. For small examples this may be fine, but for larger projects it could be frustrating for users.
Another limitation is that Shinylive is not always the right fit for apps that need secure server-side processing, private data, user accounts, databases, or heavy backend work. In those cases, a traditional Shiny app with proper hosting may be better.



Traditional Shiny is a better choice when the app needs more power, stronger hosting, private data handling, databases, user authentication, or regular business use. If the app is being used by a team or organization and needs to be reliable at scale, traditional Shiny hosting may be the safer option.
For example, a company dashboard that connects to live business data would usually make more sense as a traditional Shiny app.



Shinylive is a good choice when the goal is to share a smaller interactive app, teaching example, demonstration, or portfolio project. It works well when the data is not too large and the app does not need private server-side processing.
For example, a student portfolio could use Shinylive to show a small interactive dashboard or calculator directly inside a Quarto website.



Shinylive expands what is possible with Shiny by making it easier to run interactive R apps in the browser. Traditional Shiny is still important, especially for larger and more professional applications, but Shinylive is useful for sharing smaller apps through static websites.
The main advantage of Shinylive is that it makes interactive R work easier to publish and easier for the audience to access. The main limitation is that it may not be powerful or secure enough for every business use case.

title: Projects
text: This page highlights selected work in analytics, digital marketing, business strategy, and data visualization. These projects reflect my interest in using data, technology, and structured thinking to support better business decisions.

title: Projects
text:
Streaming Service Loyalty Research
Project Focus: Marketing research, consumer loyalty, and cancellation risk
This project examined consumer loyalty and cancellation risk among streaming service users. Streaming services operate in a competitive market where consumers can easily subscribe, cancel, or switch platforms. Because switching costs are low, companies need to understand which customers are loyal and which customers may be at risk of canceling.
Main Research Question:
What factors are associated with consumer loyalty and cancellation risk for streaming services?
Final Hypotheses:

H1: Customers with higher loyalty index scores will show lower cancellation risk.
H2: Net Promoter Score category is related to customer loyalty.
H3: Past cancellation behavior is related to current cancellation risk.
H4: Respondents who have cancelled a streaming service within the past year will have different loyalty levels than respondents who have not cancelled.



Key Measures Used

Loyalty Index
Net Promoter Score category
Cancellation risk
Past cancellation behavior
Future cancellation likelihood



Skills Demonstrated

Marketing research planning
Survey design and data cleaning
Loyalty index development
Net Promoter Score interpretation
Cancellation risk analysis
Chi-square testing
Business insight development



Analysis Summary:
The final cleaned sample included 37 valid responses. A Loyalty Index was created by averaging five loyalty statement items. Respondents were also grouped into Net Promoter Score categories as promoters, passives, or detractors. Cancellation likelihood was recoded into high risk and low/moderate risk categories.
The results showed a mean Loyalty Index score of 3.89 out of 5, suggesting moderately strong loyalty among respondents. The Net Promoter Score results were mixed but positive, with 40% promoters, 40% passives, and 20% detractors. The analysis also found that 37.8% of respondents were classified as high cancellation risk.
A chi-square test found a statistically significant relationship between past cancellation behavior and current cancellation risk. This result should be interpreted cautiously because of the small sample size, but it suggests that past cancellation behavior may be useful when identifying customers at risk of canceling.
Professional Value:
This project shows my ability to connect survey research to business decision-making. The findings can help streaming services identify at-risk subscribers earlier, monitor loyalty more effectively, and design retention strategies before customers cancel.

title: Projects

Project Focus: Digital analytics and website measurement
This project used Google Analytics 4 to explore how businesses can measure website and app behavior. The work focused on events, conversions, realtime reports, and how analytics can support marketing decisions.

title: Projects
text:
Marketing Performance Dashboard
Project Focus: Dashboard development and marketing analytics
This project used Quarto and R to build a dashboard that summarizes results from the Streaming Service Loyalty research project. The dashboard uses real survey data to display loyalty, NPS category, cancellation risk, and past cancellation behavior.
Business Question:
How can survey data be turned into a clear dashboard that supports retention-focused decision-making?

Valid survey responses
Mean Loyalty Index
Net Promoter Score category
High cancellation risk
Past cancellation behavior
Cancellation likelihood

Data visualization

Audience-facing reporting



Professional Value:
This project shows my ability to turn research data into a clear visual format that helps people compare results and make decisions.

title: Projects
text:
WallPix Innovation Project
Project Focus: Entrepreneurship and product strategy
WallPix was developed as an innovation project focused on turning personal photos into customizable wall art. The project explored value proposition, target customers, product positioning, and business model development.
Business Question:
How can a creative product idea be positioned for a specific customer need?


Key Areas Explored

Target market
Customer need

Business model planning
Creative differentiation

Creative strategy



Professional Value:

Project Focus: Professional positioning and communication
This project focused on building a stronger professional brand around strategic leadership, analytics, and digital business. It included refining profile language, identifying career direction, and communicating strengths more clearly to employers.
Business Question:
How can a professional profile communicate a clear career direction quickly and effectively?

Professional Value:

title: Kennon Holton-Tran
text:
I am a Business Administration student with an E-Business emphasis at California State Polytechnic University, Pomona. My interests sit at the intersection of strategy, analytics, technology, and digital business.
My career goal is to move into a strategic leadership role where I can help define a company’s vision, organize the structure needed to support that vision, and use data to guide better business decisions.

title: Google Analytics
text: This page highlights my work with Google Analytics 4 and how web analytics can support better marketing and business decisions.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Certification
I completed the Google Analytics Certification in 2026. This certification supports my understanding of digital measurement, website behavior, events, conversions, and marketing performance analysis.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Project Overview
For this project, I worked with Google Analytics 4, also known as GA4. GA4 is used to measure how people interact with websites and apps. It helps businesses understand where users come from, what actions they take, and whether those actions support business goals.
The main purpose of this work was to understand how analytics can connect user behavior to decision-making. Instead of only looking at traffic numbers, GA4 helps show what people actually do after arriving on a website.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Key Concepts

Data Streams
A data stream is the source of data flowing into a GA4 property. A company may have a website data stream, an app data stream, or both. This allows GA4 to collect information from different digital platforms in one place.


Events
GA4 uses events to track user interactions. Events can include actions such as page views, clicks, scrolls, form submissions, video plays, and purchases.
This is important because modern analytics is not only about counting visits. It is about understanding specific behaviors.


Conversions
Conversions are important events that connect to business goals. For example, a purchase, sign-up, lead form submission, or demo request could be marked as a conversion.
Conversions help a business measure whether its website is actually producing useful outcomes.


Realtime Reports
Realtime reports show what users are doing on a website or app in the last 30 minutes. This can help confirm whether tracking is working and whether users are currently active.
Realtime reporting is useful for testing campaigns, checking new tracking setups, and monitoring live activity.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Business Value of GA4
GA4 is valuable because it helps businesses make better decisions with actual user behavior data. A business can use GA4 to answer questions such as:

Where are visitors coming from?
Which pages are attracting attention?
Which actions are users taking?
Are users completing important goals?
Which campaigns appear to be driving useful traffic?

This kind of information can help marketing teams improve campaigns, website content, and user experience.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Example Use Case
A company running a digital marketing campaign could use GA4 to evaluate whether the campaign is working. The team could review traffic sources, user engagement, events, and conversions.
For example, if a campaign drives many visitors but very few conversions, the business may need to improve the landing page, adjust the audience targeting, or change the call to action.
If a campaign drives fewer visitors but a higher conversion rate, that traffic may be more valuable even if the overall volume is smaller.

title: Google Analytics
text:
Skills Demonstrated
This project helped me practice skills related to digital analytics and marketing measurement, including:

Navigating GA4 reports
Understanding events and conversions
Reviewing realtime user activity
Connecting website behavior to business goals
Thinking critically about marketing performance
Using analytics to support decision-making

title: Google Analytics
text:
Reflection
GA4 is useful because it helps move marketing analysis beyond surface-level metrics. Page views and traffic are helpful, but they do not tell the full story. A stronger analysis looks at what users do, whether they engage, and whether their actions support the goals of the organization.
This connects directly to my career interests because I want to work in roles that combine strategy, technology, and data. GA4 is one example of how digital tools can help leaders understand performance and make better decisions.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Shiny is an R package that allows people to build interactive web applications directly from R. Instead of only creating static reports, charts, or tables, Shiny lets the audience interact with the information. A user can filter data, choose options, change inputs, and see the results update on the page.
This is useful because many business questions are not answered with one single chart. A manager, coworker, or client may want to explore different views of the same data. Shiny makes that possible without requiring the audience to know R code.



Shiny can be used to create dashboards, data tools, reports, calculators, and interactive visualizations. In a workplace setting, a Shiny app could help a marketing team review campaign performance, compare customer segments, or test how different assumptions affect results.
For example, instead of sending a coworker a spreadsheet with many tabs, a Shiny app could let them choose a product category, date range, or customer group. The app would then update the charts and tables automatically.



A Shiny app usually has two main parts: the user interface and the server logic.
The user interface controls what the audience sees. This includes titles, text, buttons, dropdown menus, sliders, charts, and tables. The server logic controls what happens behind the scenes. It takes the user’s input, runs the needed R code, and sends the updated output back to the app.
This structure is what makes Shiny interactive. The user changes something on the page, and the app responds.



Shiny is valuable because it helps turn data analysis into something people can use directly. A regular report may explain what happened, but a Shiny app lets the audience explore the data themselves.
This can make communication clearer. Instead of creating many separate charts for every possible question, the analyst can build one app that allows users to change the view based on what they need.
Shiny is also useful for decision-making. It can help teams compare scenarios, review metrics, and focus on the information that matters most.



A marketing department could use a Shiny app to review campaign performance across different platforms. The app could include inputs for platform, campaign type, and date range. The output could show impressions, clicks, conversions, conversion rate, and cost per conversion.
This would help the team quickly compare performance without manually filtering the data each time.



Shiny is powerful, but it also has limitations. It requires some knowledge of R, and more complex apps can be harder to build and maintain. If the app is shared with many users, it may also need hosting, maintenance, and performance planning.
Another limitation is that Shiny apps are not just regular reports. They behave more like software. That means the developer needs to think about usability, errors, speed, and how the audience will interact with the app.



Shiny is a useful tool for creating interactive web applications with R. It helps analysts move beyond static reports and gives the audience a way to explore data directly. For business users, Shiny can make dashboards, decision tools, and data summaries more useful because the information can respond to the user’s needs.
